mission.title: Install and Configure vLLM
mission.description: vLLM is a high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for large language models (LLMs). It is ideal for applications requiring fast and scalable LLM serving, leveraging advanced techniques for memory management and request batching. Key trade-offs include the need for GPU resources for optimal performance and the complexity of configuration for distributed setups.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the vLLM Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install vLLM using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add vllm https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/releases/download/v0.18.0

```

> This adds the specific vLLM repository for version 0.18.0.

steps.title: Install Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for vLLM to function correctly in Kubernetes, defining the custom resources used by the application.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vllm-project/vllm/v0.18.0/deploy/crds/vllm.io_vllms_crd.yaml

```

> This installs the required CRDs for vLLM.

steps.title: Install vLLM with Helm
steps.description:
This command installs the vLLM application in the specified namespace, ensuring that all necessary components are deployed.

```bash

helm install vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --create-namespace --version 0.18.0

```

> This installs vLLM in the 'vllm' namespace.

steps.title: Create an Example Custom Resource
steps.description:
Creating a custom resource for vLLM allows you to define the model and its configuration for serving.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: example-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 2
EOF

```

> This creates an example vLLM resource for serving the GPT-3 model.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: example-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 2
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Custom Resource is Ready
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the vLLM custom resource to ensure it is running and ready to serve requests.

```bash

kubectl get vllm -n vllm

```

> You should see the status of your vLLM resource as 'Ready'.

uninstall.title: Uninstall vLLM
uninstall.description:
This step will remove vLLM and its associated resources from your Kubernetes cluster. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if any persistent volumes are used.

```bash

helm uninstall vllm --namespace vllm

kubectl delete namespace vllm

```

> This command uninstalls the vLLM release.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade vLLM
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any important data. This process will upgrade vLLM to the latest version while maintaining your configurations.

```bash

kubectl get vllm -n vllm -o yaml > vllm-backup.yaml

helm upgrade vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --version 0.18.0

kubectl get vllm -n vllm

```

> Backup the current vLLM configuration.
> This command upgrades vLLM to the specified version.
> Verify that the vLLM resource is still running and ready.
commands: kubectl get vllm -n vllm -o yaml > vllm-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'vllm not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The vLLM custom resource may not have been created correctly.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the vLLM custom resource is defined and applied correctly using the provided YAML.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate that the Kubernetes API server is unreachable.

**Fix:**
Check your kubeconfig and ensure that your Kubernetes cluster is running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have enough resources to schedule the vLLM pods.

**Fix:**
Increase the resource limits of your Kubernetes nodes or reduce the resource requests in the vLLM configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'vllm pods are in CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the vLLM pods are failing to start due to configuration issues.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the vLLM pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n vllm' to identify the issue.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new models, improved memory management, and enhanced performance metrics.
versionNotes.deprecations: No specific deprecations noted in this release.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get vllm -n vllm' with the status of your vLLM resource as 'Ready'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources and the necessary tools installed.
